questions.question: Define the terms 'memory address' and 'memory location' in the context of digital design. Explain the fundamental relationship between them.
modelAnswer: A memory address is a unique identifier, often a binary number, used to reference a specific place in memory.
A memory location is the physical storage element (e.g., a register or a cell in an array) that holds a data value.
The relationship: the address is the 'name' or pointer used to access the contents stored at the corresponding physical location.

questions.question: Describe the process a CPU undergoes to write a value to a specific memory address. Your answer should include the role of the address bus, data bus, and control signals.
modelAnswer: The CPU places the target memory address onto the address bus.
The CPU places the data value to be written onto the data bus.
The CPU activates the appropriate control signals (e.g., asserting a 'write enable' signal and deasserting a 'read enable' signal).
The memory unit decodes the address on the address bus and stores the data from the data bus into the corresponding location.

questions.question: Compare and contrast byte-addressable and word-addressable memory architectures. Discuss a key hardware implication of choosing a byte-addressable design and explain how a CPU might read a single byte from memory in such an architecture if its data bus is a full word wide.
modelAnswer: Byte-addressable: Each byte has a unique address. Word-addressable: Each word (group of bytes) has a unique address.
A key hardware implication of byte-addressability is the need for more address lines to access the same total memory capacity compared to word-addressability.
To read a single byte on a word-wide bus, the CPU reads the entire word containing the byte and then uses byte-select signals to extract the desired byte.
